screens.prompt: Case update: the burst's recording is buried under enough noise that a naive glance at the waveform tells you nothing about its carrier frequency. If the burst really does have a specific hidden carrier, would tuning a frequency dial while watching the recording's spectrum actually let you find it, even with substantial noise mixed in?
choices: No — noise this heavy makes the true carrier frequency undetectable in the spectrum
Yes — a genuine carrier still shows up as a spectral peak standing above the noise floor, even with substantial noise present
screens.reveal: Yes — this is exactly Wiener–Khinchin at work: a real periodic/oscillatory component concentrates its power at a specific frequency, while noise spreads its power thinly across the whole spectrum. Below, tune the frequency dial yourself and watch the spectral peak track your setting — find the value that matches the recording's actual hidden carrier.

screens.prompt: Sweep the frequency dial until the spectrum's peak lands on 4 Hz — the burst's suspected carrier frequency.
screens.successFeedback: There it is — a clean peak at 4 Hz, standing above the noise floor even though the waveform itself looked like a mess. This is exactly why spectral analysis, not a glance at the raw waveform, is the tool for finding a hidden periodic component.
hints: Drag toward higher frequencies from the left end of the dial — 4 Hz is roughly in the middle of the available range.
Watch the spectrum panel's dashed red marker — it tracks the currently-detected peak live as you adjust f.

screens.prompt: Why does the true carrier still produce a clear spectral peak, even with substantial noise added?

choices.text: The carrier concentrates its power at one specific frequency, while noise spreads its power thinly and roughly evenly across many frequencies
Noise has no effect whatsoever on the spectrum
choices.feedback: Noise definitely affects the spectrum — it raises the whole noise floor around the peak. It just doesn't concentrate power at one frequency the way a genuine carrier does, so the peak still stands out above that raised floor.

choices.text: The peak only appears because the recording is exactly 4 seconds long
choices.feedback: The recording length affects frequency resolution (how finely you can distinguish nearby frequencies), not whether a real spectral concentration of power shows up at all — the peak's existence comes from the carrier's genuine periodicity, not the recording's specific duration.

screens.successFeedback: Right — this is the concentrated-vs-spread-out power distinction that makes spectral analysis such a powerful detection tool, one you'll lean on heavily for the rest of this course.

screens.body: Now suppose two separate sensors both pick up the same burst, at slightly different times (one antenna is farther away). The **cross-correlation** $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t-\tau)]$ measures how strongly one process at time $t$ relates to a *different* process shifted by $\tau$ — the direct generalization of autocorrelation ($R_X(\tau)$ is just the special case $Y=X$) to a pair of related signals. If sensor $Y$'s recording is genuinely a delayed copy of sensor $X$'s, $R_{XY}(\tau)$ peaks sharply at $\tau$ equal to that time delay — giving you a direct way to measure how much later the second sensor received the same signal, from cross-correlation alone. Fourier-transforming $R_{XY}(\tau)$ over $\tau$ gives the **cross-spectral density** $S_{XY}(f)$, the frequency-domain partner of cross-correlation exactly as $S_X(f)$ partners $R_X(\tau)$.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why would R_XY(τ) peak specifically at the true time delay between two sensors receiving the same signal, rather than at τ = 0 or some other value?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that R_XY(τ) = E[X(t)Y(t−τ)] measures agreement between X(t) and a τ-shifted copy of Y. If Y is genuinely a delayed copy of X by some true delay d (Y(t) ≈ X(t−d)), then shifting Y back by exactly τ = d realigns it with X almost perfectly, producing the strongest possible correlation at that specific τ — any other shift leaves the two signals misaligned and less strongly correlated.

screens.worked: You compute $R_{XY}(\tau)$ for several candidate lags and get: $\tau=0$: 2, $\tau=1$: 3, $\tau=2$: 9, $\tau=3$: 4. The peak occurs at $\tau=2$, so your estimate of the time delay between the two sensors is 2 (in whatever time units $\tau$ was measured in).
screens.prompt: A different pair of sensors gives $R_{XY}(\tau)$ values: $\tau=0$: 5, $\tau=1$: 11, $\tau=2$: 6, $\tau=3$: 3. What is your estimate of the time delay (the $\tau$ value where the peak occurs)?
screens.successFeedback: Right — the peak value (11) occurs at τ = 1, so that's the estimated delay between the two sensors' recordings of the same underlying signal.
hints: Find which τ value gives the largest R_XY(τ).
Compare 5, 11, 6, 3 — which is the biggest, and at which τ?
